title: Toxoplasma Infections
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations

items.title: Understanding Toxoplasmosis: Key Clinical Manifestations and Risks
items.content: Toxoplasmosis, caused by the parasite T. gondii, often remains asymptomatic in immunocompetent individuals but can lead to serious conditions such as encephalitis in immunocompromised patients. Congenital toxoplasmosis occurs via transplacental transmission and may result in chorioretinitis or neurodevelopmental issues.
keyPoints: Acute infection is typically asymptomatic in immunocompetent individuals but can cause systemic or ocular disease.
Encephalitis is a classic presentation in immunocompromised individuals, including HIV-positive patients and transplant recipients.
Congenital toxoplasmosis may lead to chorioretinitis, strabismus, epilepsy, and psychomotor retardation.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission Pathways of Toxoplasma gondii
items.content: T. gondii is transmitted to humans through ingestion of tissue cysts in undercooked meat or sporulated oocysts from contaminated food or water. The parasite undergoes a life cycle involving tachyzoites and bradyoites, with the latter forming tissue cysts that persist in host tissues.
keyPoints: Tissue cysts containing bradyoites are transmitted via undercooked meat.
Sporulated oocysts from feline feces can contaminate food or water sources.
Tachyzoites, which replicate rapidly, can disseminate throughout the body.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Geographical Variations in Toxoplasma gondii Virulence and Clinical Implications
items.content: T. gondii strains vary by geography, with South and Central American strains often being more virulent and associated with symptomatic disease such as ocular posterior uveitis. Travel history to these regions should prompt consideration of toxoplasmosis in patients presenting with severe symptoms.
keyPoints: Strains from South and Central America are typically more virulent, often leading to symptomatic disease.
Ocular toxoplasmosis is a significant concern for individuals from or traveling to these regions.
Severe disease manifestations like sepsis and pneumonia should be considered in patients with relevant travel history.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Seroprevalence Trends in Toxoplasma Infections
items.content: The seroprevalence of T. gondii varies by region and age, with notable differences between the United States/Europe and regions like Brazil.
keyPoints: In the US, 11% of individuals >6 years old had evidence of Toxoplasma exposure (2011–2014).
Foreign-born Americans have higher seroprevalence rates.
Seroprevalence can be as high as 78% in Brazil.
Increased awareness has decreased prevalence worldwide, but it remains high in Central and South America.

items.title: Routes of Transmission for Toxoplasma Infection
items.content: Toxoplasma gondii is primarily transmitted orally, but can also spread through blood or organs and transplacentally.
keyPoints: Oral transmission via sporulated oocysts from contaminated sources (soil, food, water).
Cats excrete up to 100 million oocysts per day during acute infection.
Transmission can occur through undercooked meat or untreated water/shellfish.
Blood/organ transmission possible in transplants and blood transfusions.
Transplacental transmission risk is highest if maternal infection occurs <6 months before conception.

items.title: Impact of Gestational Timing on Congenital Toxoplasmosis Risk
items.content: The timing of T. gondii infection during pregnancy significantly influences the risk and severity of congenital transmission.
keyPoints: One-third of pregnant women with acute infection transmit to fetus.
Infection <6 months before conception increases transplacental risk.
First trimester infections have ~15% transmission rate but severe neonatal disease.
Third trimester infections have 65% transmission rate, often asymptomatic at birth.
Infants infected in utero may develop learning disabilities or chronic neurologic issues.

items.title: Mechanism of Toxoplasma Dissemination and Immune Response in Immunocompetent Hosts
items.content: Upon ingestion, bradyoites or sporozoites from tissue cysts or oocysts are released. Bradyoites invade the gastrointestinal tract, transforming into tachyzoites that disseminate to various organs. In immunocompetent hosts, both humoral and cellular immune responses control infection through mechanisms like parasiticidal antibodies, macrophage activation, IFN-γ production, and CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocyte stimulation.
keyPoints: Bradyoites invade the gastrointestinal tract and transform into tachyzoites.
Dissemination occurs to organs such as lymphatic tissue, skeletal muscle, myocardium, retina, placenta, and CNS.
Immune control involves parasiticidal antibodies, macrophage activation, IFN-γ production, and CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes.

items.title: Persistence and Clinical Implications of Toxoplasma Infection in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: In immunocompromised or fetal hosts, the lack of immune control allows tachyzoite persistence, leading to progressive organ destruction. Persistent infection with bradyoites is lifelong and subclinical but can cause recrudescent infections when immunity wanes. Chronic T. gondii infection may influence neuropsychiatric conditions, though its contribution remains controversial.
keyPoints: Immunocompromised hosts experience persistent tachyzoite infection leading to organ destruction.
Lifelong subclinical bradyoite cysts can cause recrudescent infections in immunocompromised individuals.
Chronic T. gondii infection is hypothesized to contribute to neuropsychiatric conditions, but evidence remains controversial.

items.title: Toxoplasma Infections: CNS Pathology and Implications in AIDS Patients
items.content: In the central nervous system (CNS), Toxoplasma gondii causes both focal and diffuse meningoencephalitis, characterized by necrosis and microglial nodules. Necrotizing encephalitis presents with small diffuse lesions and perivascular cuffing in immunocompetent individuals, while polymorphonuclear leukocytes may also be present in AIDS patients. The presence of bradyzoite-containing cysts near necrotic tissue is notable.
keyPoints: CNS involvement includes focal and diffuse meningoencephalitis with necrosis and microglial nodules.
Necrotizing encephalitis shows small diffuse lesions with perivascular cuffing in immunocompetent patients.
In AIDS patients, polymorphonuclear leukocytes may accompany monocytes, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.
Bradyzoite-containing cysts are often found near necrotic tissue.
ART has decreased the incidence of toxoplasmosis in developed countries.

items.title: Toxoplasma Infections: Pulmonary and Cardiac Pathology in AIDS Patients
items.content: In patients with AIDS who die from toxoplasmosis, 40–70% have lung and heart involvement. Interstitial pneumonitis can develop in neonates and immunocompromised individuals, characterized by thickened alveolar septa infiltrated with mononuclear and plasma cells. Tachyzoites and bradyoite-containing cysts may be observed within the alveolar membrane. Cardiac involvement includes cysts and parasite aggregates in cardiac muscle tissue, associated with focal necrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration.
keyPoints: 40–70% of AIDS patients who die from toxoplasmosis have lung and heart involvement.
Interstitial pneumonitis is characterized by thickened alveolar septa infiltrated with mononuclear and plasma cells.
Tachyzoites and bradyoite-containing cysts can be found within the alveolar membrane.
Cardiac muscle tissue may show cysts, aggregates of parasites, focal necrosis, and inflammatory cell infiltration.

items.title: Toxoplasma Infections: Ocular Pathology in Immunocompetent Individuals
items.content: In the eye, Toxoplasma gondii can cause monocyte, lymphocyte, and plasma cell infiltrates leading to uni- or multifocal lesions. Granulomatous lesions and retinochoroiditis may occur after acute necrotizing retinitis. Other ocular complications include iridocyclitis, cataracts, and glaucoma. T. gondii is the most common cause of posterior uveitis in immunocompetent individuals.
keyPoints: Ocular infiltrates may produce uni- or multifocal lesions.
Granulomatous lesions and retinochoroiditis can follow acute necrotizing retinitis.
Complications include iridocyclitis, cataracts, and glaucoma.
T. gondii is the most common cause of posterior uveitis in immunocompetent individuals.

items.title: Role of Antibodies in Toxoplasma Infection
items.content: In immunocompetent hosts, acute Toxoplasma infection triggers the production of specific antibodies that play a crucial role in immune defense. IgM and IgG antibodies are rapidly induced post-infection, with IgG being parasiticidal in vitro when complement is present.
keyPoints: IgA antibody directed at tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1 is a marker for congenital and acute toxoplasmosis.
Monoclonal gammopathy of the IgG class can occur in congenitally infected infants.
Increased IgM levels may be seen in newborns with congenital infection.
Polyclonal IgG antibodies are parasiticidal in vitro with serum complement.
Antibody titers are not useful for establishing reactivation or following activity of infection in immunocompromised patients.

items.title: Cell-Mediated Immunity and Toxoplasma Infection
items.content: Cell-mediated immunity is the primary protective response against Toxoplasma gondii. This involves activation of macrophages, T-cell responses, and cytokine production, particularly IL-12 and IFN-gamma, which are crucial for controlling parasite growth.
keyPoints: Macrophages activated by antibody-opsonized parasites play a key role in defense.
CD4+ and CD8+ T cells produce lymphokines that expand the immune cell repertoire.
$T_{H}1$ response, driven by IL-12 and IFN-gamma, is essential for controlling parasite growth.
IL-18, IL-7, and IL-15 upregulate IFN-gamma production during infection.
In AIDS patients with toxoplasmosis reactivation, T cells fail to secrete IFN-gamma and IL-2, contributing to persistent infection.

items.title: Diagnosis Challenges in Acute Toxoplasmosis
items.content: Acute toxoplasmosis often goes unrecognized due to its asymptomatic nature, particularly in immunocompetent individuals. This poses a diagnostic challenge, especially for pregnant women where the infection might be acquired during pregnancy.
keyPoints: 80–90% of adults and children with acute toxoplasmosis are asymptomatic
Diagnosis is difficult in mothers infected during pregnancy due to asymptomatic nature

items.title: Clinical Presentation in Immunocompetent Patients
items.content: In immunocompetent patients, acute toxoplasmosis commonly presents with cervical lymphadenopathy and may include symptoms like headache, malaise, fatigue, and fever. Rare complications can occur due to more virulent strains.
keyPoints: Cervical lymphadenopathy is the most common manifestation
Generalized lymphadenopathy occurs in 20–30% of symptomatic patients
Headache, malaise, fatigue, and fever (<40°C [<104°F]) occur in 20-40% with lymphadenopathy
Rare complications include pneumonia, myocarditis, encephalopathy, pericarditis, and polymyositis

items.title: Toxoplasmosis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Immunocompromised patients are at high risk for severe toxoplasmosis, particularly affecting the CNS. Reactivation of latent infection is common when CD4+ T-cell count falls below 100/μL.
keyPoints: >95% of Toxoplasma encephalitis (TE) in AIDS patients are due to reactivation
Encephalitis develops when CD4+ T-cell count <100/μL
TE was a major opportunistic infection before effective ART, affecting 15-40% of seropositive adult AIDS patients

items.title: Key Clinical Manifestations of Toxoplasma Encephalitis
items.content: Toxoplasma encephalitis presents with a range of neurological symptoms, including altered mental status, seizures, and focal neurologic findings. The infection can lead to diffuse cortical dysfunction progressing to focal deficits as it advances.
keyPoints: Altered mental status occurs in 75% of cases.
Fever ranges from 10–72%.
Seizures are seen in 33%.
Headaches occur in 56%.
Focal neurologic findings appear in 60%, including motor deficits, cranial nerve palsies, movement disorders, dysmetria, visual-field loss, and aphasia.
Lesions often involve the brainstem, basal ganglia, pituitary gland, and corticomedullary junction.
CSF may show a modest increase in cell count and protein concentration but not glucose.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Cerebral Toxoplasmosis
items.content: Cerebral toxoplasmosis must be differentiated from other CNS infections or tumors in AIDS patients, including herpes simplex encephalitis and primary CNS lymphoma.
keyPoints: Differentials include herpes simplex encephalitis, cryptococcal meningitis, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, and primary CNS lymphoma.
Pituitary involvement can lead to panhypopituitarism and hyponatremia from inappropriate vasopressin secretion.

items.title: Congenital Toxoplasmosis: Incidence and Outcomes
items.content: Congenital toxoplasmosis affects a significant number of infants annually, with outcomes dependent on timely diagnosis and treatment.
keyPoints: 400 to 4000 infants in the U.S. are affected each year.
Acute maternal infection is usually asymptomatic; prenatal serologic screening aids diagnosis.
Severity decreases as gestation progresses, but risk of reactivation persists.
Severe disabilities linked to delayed diagnosis and treatment, neonatal complications, and uncorrected hydrocephalus.
With appropriate treatment, up to 70% have normal developmental outcomes.

items.title: Ocular Manifestations in Congenital and AIDS-related Toxoplasmosis
items.content: Toxoplasma infections can lead to various ocular manifestations, including blurred vision, scotoma, photophobia, eye pain, macular involvement with loss of central vision, nystagmus due to poor fixation, disorders of convergence, and strabismus. In congenital cases, ophthalmologic examination is crucial for newborns suspected of infection. As inflammation resolves, vision may improve but episodic flares of chorioretinitis can lead to progressive retinal tissue destruction and glaucoma. Lesions typically appear near the posterior pole of the retina as yellow-white patches with indistinct margins that evolve into white plaques with distinct borders and black spots within the retinal pigment. In AIDS patients, lesions are larger with diffuse necrosis and contain tachyzoites and bradyoite cysts.
keyPoints: Ocular manifestations include blurred vision, scotoma, photophobia, eye pain, macular involvement, nystagmus, disorders of convergence, strabismus
Ophthalmologic examination recommended for newborns with suspected congenital infection
Episodic flares of chorioretinitis can lead to progressive retinal tissue destruction and glaucoma
Lesions near the posterior pole: yellow-white patches evolving into white plaques with distinct borders and black spots within the retinal pigment
In AIDS, lesions are large with diffuse necrosis containing tachyzoites and bradyoite cysts
Toxoplasmic chorioretinitis may precede encephalitis

items.title: PCR Utility in Acute Toxoplasmosis Diagnosis
items.content: PCR is the primary method for detecting T. gondii organisms in tissue or biological fluids, crucial for diagnosing acute toxoplasmosis.
keyPoints: PCR does not rule out toxoplasmosis if negative.
Isolation of T. gondii from body fluids indicates acute infection; from biopsied tissue indicates presence of cysts.

items.title: Serologic Testing for Acute Toxoplasmosis
items.content: Acute toxoplasmosis is diagnosed by detecting IgG and IgM antibodies simultaneously in serum, with IgA presence supporting acute infection.
keyPoints: Positive IgG titers (>1:10) appear 2–3 weeks post-infection.
IgM should be measured alongside IgG to determine infection timing.
Double-sandwich IgM-ELISA or IgM-ISAGA are recommended for specificity and sensitivity.

items.title: Serologic Diagnosis in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: In HIV-infected patients, serologic testing for IgG antibody to Toxoplasma is crucial soon after HIV diagnosis to predict latent infection.
keyPoints: Predictive value of clinical criteria for toxoplasmic encephalitis (TE) in AIDS patients can be as high as 80%.
Over 97% of AIDS patients with TE have IgG antibody to T. gondii; IgM is usually not detectable.
Serologic evidence often precedes the development of TE.

items.title: Imaging in Toxoplasma Encephalitis: When to Use CT vs. MRI
items.content: CT and MRI are both used for diagnosing toxoplasmic encephalitis (TE), with double-dose contrast CT identifying single or multiple lesions (<2 cm). MRI is more sensitive, showing multiple lesions in both hemispheres, especially the basal ganglia and corticomedullary junction. However, findings are not pathognomonic due to similarities with CNS lymphomas.
keyPoints: Double-dose contrast CT can identify single or multiple contrast-enhancing lesions (<2 cm).
MRI shows multiple lesions in both hemispheres, particularly the basal ganglia and corticomedullary junction.
Findings are not pathognomonic; 40% of CNS lymphomas are multifocal, and 50% are ring-enhancing.
False-negative rates for MRI and CT scans are ~10%.
A single lesion on MRI increases the likelihood of primary CNS lymphoma.

items.title: Therapeutic Trial to Differentiate TE from Lymphoma
items.content: A therapeutic trial with anti-Toxoplasma medications can help differentiate TE from CNS lymphoma. Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine or clindamycin leads to clinical improvement in >50% of patients by day 3, and >90% show improvement by day 7. Lack of response suggests lymphoma, necessitating a brain biopsy.
keyPoints: Treatment with pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine or clindamycin results in >50% clinical improvement by day 3.
>90% of treated patients show improvement by day 7.
Lack of response or worsening symptoms by day 7 suggests lymphoma, requiring brain biopsy.

items.title: Diagnosing Congenital Toxoplasmosis in Newborns
items.content: In congenital toxoplasmosis, PCR analysis of amniotic fluid is preferred over fetal blood sampling. Diagnosis relies on IgG persistence or positive IgM after the first week of life. Up to 25% of infected newborns may be seronegative with normal exams, necessitating eye and brain assessments.
keyPoints: PCR analysis of amniotic fluid is preferred for diagnosing congenital toxoplasmosis.
Serologic diagnosis involves IgG persistence or positive IgM after the first week of life.
Up to 25% of infected newborns may be seronegative and have normal physical exams.

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS
items.title: Treatment Protocols for Congenital Toxoplasmosis in Neonates
items.content: Neonates with congenital toxoplasmosis are treated with a regimen of daily oral pyrimethamine (1 mg/kg), sulfadiazine (100 mg/kg), and folinic acid for one year. Prednisone may be added depending on clinical signs and symptoms.
keyPoints: Daily treatment includes pyrimethamine 1 mg/kg, sulfadiazine 100 mg/kg, and folinic acid for 1 year.
Prednisone (1 mg/kg per day) may be used based on clinical presentation.
Prenatal antibiotic therapy is offered to reduce severity of infection in infants if pregnancy is not terminated.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS
items.title: Management of Ocular Toxoplasmosis in Immunocompetent Patients
items.content: Immunocompetent adults and children with ocular toxoplasmosis are treated for 6 weeks using pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine or clindamycin, sometimes with prednisone. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is an alternative if pyrimethamine is unavailable.
keyPoints: Treatment duration for ocular toxoplasmosis is 6 weeks with pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine or clindamycin.
Prednisone may be added to the treatment regimen.
TMP-SMX (5 mg/kg bid based on TMP) can replace pyrimethamine if needed.
Ocular disease may resolve without treatment but is treated for severe cases or those near fovea/optic disc.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.title: Treatment of Acute Toxoplasmosis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Acute toxoplasmosis is rapidly fatal if untreated in immunocompromised patients, such as those with AIDS or transplant recipients. The recommended treatment involves pyrimethamine (200 mg load, 50 mg/d for <60 kg, 75 mg/d for >60 kg), sulfadiazine (1000 mg qid for <60 kg; 1500 mg qid for >60 kg), and leucovorin (10–25 mg/d) to reduce hematologic toxicity. This regimen should be continued for a minimum of 6 weeks. In cases of sulfa intolerance, clindamycin (600 mg qid) can be used as an alternative.
keyPoints: Pyrimethamine dosage: 200 mg load, then 50 mg/d if <60 kg or 75 mg/d if >60 kg
Sulfadiazine dosage: 1000 mg qid for <60 kg; 1500 mg qid for >60 kg
Leucovorin to reduce hematologic toxicity: 10–25 mg/d
Minimum treatment duration: 6 weeks
Alternative for sulfa intolerance: Clindamycin (600 mg qid)
TMP-SMX is an effective alternative in resource-poor settings
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.title: Primary Prophylaxis for Toxoplasmic Encephalitis (TE) in AIDS Patients
items.content: AIDS patients who are seropositive for T. gondii and have a CD4+ T lymphocyte count of <100/μL should receive prophylaxis against TE. The daily dose of TMP-SMX (one double-strength tablet qd), used for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis, is effective against TE. Alternatives include dapsone-pyrimethamine or atovaquone with or without pyrimethamine.
keyPoints: Indication for prophylaxis: CD4+ T lymphocyte count <100/μL
Prophylactic regimen: TMP-SMX (one double-strength tablet qd)
Alternatives: Dapsone-pyrimethamine, atovaquone with or without pyrimethamine
Monotherapy options like dapsone, azithromycin, clarithromycin, or aerosolized pentamidine are insufficient
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.title: Discontinuing Primary Prophylaxis for TE in HIV-Positive Patients
items.content: Prophylaxis against TE can be discontinued in HIV-positive patients who have responded to ART and whose CD4+ T lymphocyte count has been >200/μL for 3 months. Prophylaxis should be reintroduced if the CD4+ T lymphocyte count decreases to <100–200/μL.
keyPoints: Criteria for discontinuing prophylaxis: CD4+ T lymphocyte count >200/μL for 3 months
Prophylaxis should be resumed if CD4+ T lymphocyte count drops below 100–200/μL
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.title: Preventing Toxoplasma Infection in Immunocompromised and Pregnant Individuals
items.content: To reduce the risk of primary Toxoplasma infection, seronegative immunocompromised or pregnant individuals should avoid undercooked meats and oocyst-contaminated materials. Proper cooking temperatures for various meats are crucial: lamb, beef, pork, and venison at 63°C (145°F) with a rest period; ground meat at 71°C (145°F); poultry at 74°C (165°F). Additionally, freezing meats to -20°C (-4°F) can kill cysts. Hand hygiene after gardening and thorough washing of fruits and vegetables are also recommended.
keyPoints: Cook lamb, beef, pork, venison to 63°C (145°F), rest for 3 min
Ground meat should be cooked to 71°C (145°F)
Poultry should reach 74°C (165°F)
Freezing meats at -20°C (-4°F) kills cysts
Wash hands after gardening and thoroughly wash fruits/vegetables

items.title: Preventive Measures for Cat Owners to Avoid Toxoplasma Infection
items.content: Cat owners, especially those who are immunocompromised or pregnant, should take specific precautions. Litter boxes should be cleaned daily by an HIV-negative, nonpregnant person if possible, as fresh oocysts are not infectious until they sporulate. Keeping cats indoors and feeding them only canned, dried commercial food, or well-cooked table food reduces the risk of infection. Patients do not need to part with their cats or have them tested for toxoplasmosis.
keyPoints: Clean litter boxes daily by an HIV-negative, nonpregnant person
Keep cats indoors and avoid handling stray cats
Feed cats canned/dried commercial food or well-cooked table food

items.question: Which of the following is a typical presentation of toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised individuals?
items.explanation: The classic presentation of toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised individuals is encephalitis.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: How are tissue cysts containing bradyoites typically transmitted to humans?
items.explanation: Tissue cysts containing bradyoites are transmitted in undercooked meat.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary site where tissue cysts persist in a chronic infection?
items.explanation: Tissue cysts occur principally within the central nervous system (CNS) and muscle.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which genotype of T. gondii is more frequently associated with symptomatic disease in South America?
items.explanation: Strains found in South and Central America are often of the type I virulent genotype.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which factor is associated with a low prevalence of T. gondii infection?
items.explanation: Hot arid climatic conditions are associated with a low prevalence of T. gondii infection.

items.question: What is the primary route of human Toxoplasma infection?
items.explanation: Most cases of human Toxoplasma infection are acquired by the oral route.

items.question: What is a significant source of T. gondii infection in the developed world?
items.explanation: Undercooking or insufficient freezing of meat is an important source of infection in the developed world.

items.question: What percentage of women who acquire T. gondii during pregnancy transmit it to their fetus?
items.explanation: About one-third (approximately 33%) of all women who acquire infection with T. gondii during pregnancy transmit the parasite to the fetus.

items.question: Which cells are primarily involved in controlling the infection by secreting parasiticidal antibodies and activating macrophages with radical intermediates?
items.explanation: CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes are involved in controlling infection by secreting parasiticidal antibodies and activating macrophages.

items.question: Where do tissue cysts containing bradyzoites typically appear after the acute phase of Toxoplasma infection?
items.explanation: Tissue cysts containing bradyzoites typically appear in the CNS, skeletal muscle, and retina.

items.question: What is a probable source of recrudescent infection in immunocompromised individuals?
options: Subclinical cycles of cyst ruptures followed by new bradyoite-containing cysts
Chronic inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract
Direct transmission from an infected host
items.explanation: Subclinical cycles of cyst ruptures followed by development of new bradyoite-containing cysts are a probable source of recrudescent infection.

items.question: Which staining method can reveal Toxoplasma tachyzoites in inflammatory lesions?
options: Immunofluorescent staining with parasitic antigen-specific antibodies
items.explanation: Tachyzoites are rarely visualized by routine histopathologic staining but can be revealed using immunofluorescent staining with parasitic antigen-specific antibodies.

items.question: In which condition do bradyoite-containing cysts remain relatively immunologically quiescent?
options: During the early stages of development
After reaching maturity within the brain matrix
items.explanation: Once bradyoite-containing cysts reach maturity, they remain relatively immunologically quiescent within the brain matrix.

items.question: What is a common ocular complication of Toxoplasma gondii infection in immunocompetent individuals?
items.explanation: T. gondii is the most common cause of posterior uveitis in immunocompetent individuals.

items.question: What percentage of AIDS patients who die from toxoplasmosis have involvement of the lungs and heart?
items.explanation: Among patients with AIDS who die of toxoplasmosis, 40–70% have involvement of the lungs and heart.

items.question: Which antibody is a useful marker for congenital and acute toxoplasmosis?
options: IgG directed at the tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1
IgA directed at the tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1
IgM directed at the tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1
Secretory IgE directed at the tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1
items.explanation: Titers of serum IgA antibody directed at the tachyzoite surface antigen p30/SAG1 are a useful marker for congenital and acute toxoplasmosis.

items.question: Which immune response is considered the major protective mechanism against Toxoplasma gondii during host infection?
items.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity is the major protective response evoked by the parasite during host infection.

items.question: In patients with AIDS, what alteration in immune cytokine production contributes to the persistence of Toxoplasma gondii infection?
options: Increased secretion of IL-4 and IL-10
Failure to secrete IFN-γ and IL-2
items.explanation: T cells from AIDS patients with reactivation of toxoplasmosis fail to secrete both IFN-γ and IL-2.

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is most common in immunocompetent patients with acute toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: The most common manifestation of acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent patients is cervical lymphadenopathy.

items.question: What percentage of symptomatic immunocompetent patients with toxoplasmosis may experience generalized lymphadenopathy?
items.explanation: Generalized lymphadenopathy occurs in 20–30% of symptomatic patients with toxoplasmosis.

items.question: Which laboratory finding is NOT typically associated with acute toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Routine laboratory studies usually show minimal lymphocytosis, elevated ESR, and nominal increase in serum aminotransferase levels; thrombocytopenia is not mentioned.

items.question: In immunocompromised patients with toxoplasmosis, what is the primary site of clinical manifestations?
items.explanation: The signs and symptoms of acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients principally involve the CNS.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common clinical manifestation of cerebral toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Meningeal involvement is uncommon in toxoplasma encephalitis.

items.question: What percentage of patients with cerebral toxoplasmosis may present with headaches?
items.explanation: 56% of patients with cerebral toxoplasmosis may present with headaches.

items.question: Which area is NOT commonly involved in cerebral toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: The cerebellum is not mentioned as a common site of involvement.

items.question: Which condition can result from pituitary gland involvement in toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Involvement of the pituitary gland can lead to panhypopituitarism.

items.question: Which ocular manifestation is commonly associated with macular involvement in toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Macular involvement leads to loss of central vision.

items.question: What is a common feature seen on ophthalmologic examination in toxoplasmic chorioretinitis?
items.explanation: Ophthalmologic examination reveals yellow-white, cotton-like patches.

items.question: Where are the lesions typically located in toxoplasmic chorioretinitis?
options: Near the anterior pole of the retina
Near the posterior pole of the retina
items.explanation: Lesions are usually located near the posterior pole of the retina.

items.question: Which test is considered the mainstay for detecting T. gondii in tissue or biological fluids?
items.explanation: PCR is the primary method for detecting T. gondii in tissues and body fluids.

items.question: What does isolation of T. gondii from biopsied tissue indicate?
items.explanation: Isolation from biopsied tissue indicates the presence of tissue cysts, not acute infection.

items.question: What serologic finding is indicative of an acute T. gondii infection?
options: Simultaneous presence of IgG and IgM antibodies
items.explanation: Acute infection is indicated by the simultaneous presence of IgG and IgM antibodies.

items.question: Which serologic test is more sensitive for detecting congenital toxoplasmosis in newborns?
items.explanation: The double-sandwich IgA-ELISA is more sensitive than the IgM-ELISA for detecting congenital infection.

items.question: Which imaging modality is more sensitive for evaluating the efficacy of therapy in toxoplasmosis encephalitis?
items.explanation: MRI provides a more sensitive evaluation of the efficacy of therapy than does CT.

items.question: What is the likelihood of identifying organisms in brain biopsy for T. gondii infection?
items.explanation: Brain biopsy for T. gondii identifies organisms in 50–75% of cases.

items.question: What is the recommended method to assess fetal infection with T. gondii during pregnancy?
items.explanation: PCR analysis of the amniotic fluid has replaced fetal blood sampling for assessing fetal infection.

items.question: Which test is used to confirm toxoplasmosis in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)?
items.explanation: PCR amplification of CSF may confirm toxoplasmosis.

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS
items.question: Which combination of drugs is typically used to treat congenitally infected neonates for one year?
items.explanation: Neonates are treated with daily oral pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and folinic acid for one year.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS
items.question: What is the treatment regimen for pregnant women who seroconvert after 18 weeks of pregnancy or have documented fetal infection?
items.explanation: Women who seroconvert after 18 weeks or have documented fetal infection are treated with pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine/folinic acid.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.question: For how long is ocular toxoplasmosis typically treated in immunocompetent adults?
items.explanation: Ocular toxoplasmosis is usually treated for 6 weeks with pyrimethamine plus either sulfadiazine or clindamycin.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients?
items.explanation: The recommended treatment for acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients is pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and leucovorin.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.question: Which alternative can be used for treatment of toxoplasmic encephalitis (TE) in resource-poor settings?
items.explanation: In resource-poor settings, TMP-SMX is an effective alternative for the treatment of TE.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.question: When can prophylaxis against toxoplasmic encephalitis (TE) be discontinued in HIV-positive patients?
options: When CD4+ T lymphocyte count is >200/μL for 1 month
When CD4+ T lymphocyte count is <100/μL for 3 months
When CD4+ T lymphocyte count is >200/μL for 3 months
After initial therapy for TE regardless of CD4+ count
items.explanation: Prophylaxis against TE can be discontinued when the CD4+ T lymphocyte count has been >200/μL for 3 months.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.question: Which prophylactic regimen is recommended if TMP-SMX cannot be tolerated in AIDS patients?
items.explanation: If TMP-SMX cannot be tolerated, the recommended alternative is dapsone-pyrimethamine.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended method to reduce the risk of Toxoplasma infection from meat?
options: Cook lamb, beef, pork, and venison to an internal temperature of 63°C (145°F) and rest for 3 minutes.
Wash hands after handling raw meat.
items.explanation: Cooking lamb, beef, pork, and venison to an internal temperature of 63°C (145°F) and resting for 3 minutes kills Toxoplasma cysts.

items.question: What is a recommended practice regarding cat litter boxes to prevent Toxoplasma infection?
options: Have an HIV-negative, nonpregnant person clean it daily.
Dispose of the litter in outdoor compost.
items.explanation: Cleaning or changing the litter box daily by an HIV-negative, nonpregnant person reduces exposure to Toxoplasma oocysts.

items.question: Which of these is a preventive measure for immunocompromised individuals regarding blood transfusions?
options: Blood should be screened for antibodies to T. gondii.
items.explanation: Ideally, blood intended for Toxoplasma-seronegative immunocompromised individuals should be screened for antibodies to T. gondii.

items.statement: Tachyzoites can infect red blood cells.
items.explanation: Tachyzoites can infect and replicate in all mammalian cells except red blood cells.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Ocular toxoplasmosis should be considered in a person from Central or South America with ocular symptoms.
items.explanation: Ocular toxoplasmosis is associated with strains found in Central and South America.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: The seroprevalence of T. gondii in the United States has increased over recent decades.
items.explanation: Seroprevalence of T. gondii in the United States has steadily decreased.

items.statement: Refrigerated anticoagulated blood can be a source of T. gondii infection.
items.explanation: Viable parasites can be cultured from refrigerated anticoagulated blood, posing an infection risk.

items.statement: In immunocompromised or fetal hosts, the immune system can control the spread of tachyzoite infection.
items.explanation: Immunocompromised or fetal hosts lack the necessary immune factors to control the spread of tachyzoite infection.

items.statement: Toxoplasma secretes signaling molecules that modulate host gene expression and immune response.
items.explanation: Toxoplasma secretes signaling molecules into infected host cells, affecting host gene expression and immune response.

items.statement: Tachyzoites can be visualized by routine histopathologic staining in inflammatory lesions.
items.explanation: Tachyzoites are rarely visualized by routine histopathologic staining but can be revealed using immunofluorescent staining.

items.statement: In the CNS, toxoplasmosis in AIDS patients is characterized by necrotizing encephalitis with small diffuse lesions and perivascular cuffing.
items.explanation: Necrotizing encephalitis in AIDS patients involves small diffuse lesions with perivascular cuffing.

items.statement: Antibody titers are useful in establishing reactivation of Toxoplasma gondii infection in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Antibody titers are not useful in establishing reactivation or following the activity of infection in immunocompromised states.

items.statement: IL-12 and IFN-γ play an essential role in controlling Toxoplasma gondii growth in the host.
items.explanation: T. gondii is a potent inducer of a TH1 phenotype, with IL-12 and IFN-γ playing an essential role in the control of the parasites' growth.

items.statement: In persons with intact immune systems, acute toxoplasmosis is usually symptomatic and self-limiting.
items.explanation: Acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent individuals is usually asymptomatic and self-limited.

items.statement: PCR amplification of Toxoplasma DNA target sequence in CSF is sensitive for active toxoplasmosis.
items.explanation: PCR amplification of the Toxoplasma DNA target sequence in CSF is specific but not sensitive for active toxoplasmosis.

items.statement: Toxoplasma encephalitis is commonly associated with meningeal involvement.
items.explanation: Meningeal involvement is uncommon in toxoplasma encephalitis.

items.statement: Congenital toxoplasmosis can result in chorioretinitis decades after initial infection.
items.explanation: Persistence of T. gondii can lead to reactivation and clinical disease, most frequently chorioretinitis.

items.statement: Toxoplasmic chorioretinitis lesions in patients with AIDS often include both free tachyzoites and cysts containing bradyoites.
items.explanation: In patients with AIDS, retinal lesions can contain both free tachyzoites and cysts.

items.statement: Vision improvement in toxoplasmic chorioretinitis is permanent without any episodic flares.
items.explanation: As inflammation resolves, vision improves but episodic flares of chorioretinitis are common.

items.statement: A negative PCR result rules out toxoplasmosis.
items.explanation: A negative PCR does not rule out toxoplasmosis.

items.statement: IgM serum antibody is usually detectable in patients with AIDS and toxoplasmosis.
items.explanation: IgM serum antibody is usually not detectable in these patients.

items.statement: A single lesion on an MRI scan is more indicative of primary CNS lymphoma than toxoplasma encephalitis.
items.explanation: The finding of a single lesion increases the likelihood of primary CNS lymphoma.

items.statement: A negative PCR result in ocular samples definitively rules out the diagnosis of ocular toxoplasmosis.
items.explanation: Negative PCR does not rule out the diagnosis of ocular toxoplasmosis.

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS
items.statement: Pregnant women with acute toxoplasmosis early in pregnancy are typically treated with pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine/folinic acid.
items.explanation: They are usually treated with spiramycin, not pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine/folinic acid.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.statement: Immunocompetent adults with only lymphadenopathy require specific therapy.
items.explanation: They do not require specific therapy unless they have persistent, severe symptoms.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · CONGENITAL INFECTION | INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPETENT PATIENTS

items.statement: Prophylaxis against TE should be reintroduced if the CD4+ T lymphocyte count decreases to <200/μL.
items.explanation: Secondary prophylaxis for TE should be reintroduced if the CD4+ T lymphocyte count falls below 200/μL.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.statement: Prophylactic monotherapy with dapsone is sufficient against toxoplasmic encephalitis.
items.explanation: Prophylactic monotherapy with dapsone alone is probably insufficient against TE.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.statement: To prevent Toxoplasma infection, it is recommended that patients part with their cats.
items.explanation: Patients need not be advised to part with their cats; instead, preventive measures should be taken.

items.statement: Freezing meats at -20°C (-4°F) is effective in killing Toxoplasma cysts.
items.explanation: Freezing meats to -20°C (-4°F) kills Toxoplasma cysts, reducing the risk of infection.

items.assertion: T. gondii can cause severe disease including sepsis and pneumonia.
items.reason: Strains from South or Central America are more virulent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the only explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: The sexual stage of T. gondii occurs in humans.
items.reason: Humans are intermediate hosts where T. gondii completes its life cycle.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the sexual stage occurs in cats, not humans.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · Toxoplasma Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Women infected with T. gondii before pregnancy are usually protected against acute infection.
items.reason: Seropositive women have immunity that prevents them from acquiring new infections during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: If maternal infection occurs in the third trimester, transplacental infection is least likely.
items.reason: The incidence of transplacental infection is greatest during the third trimester.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; however, the reason is true as the incidence is indeed highest in the third trimester.

items.assertion: In the CNS, toxoplasmosis can lead to focal and diffuse meningoencephalitis.
items.reason: This is due to evidence of necrosis and microglial nodules in affected areas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the presence of necrosis and microglial nodules explains the meningoencephalitis.

items.assertion: In patients with AIDS, pericarditis is commonly associated with toxoplasmosis.
items.reason: This association occurs due to cysts and aggregates of parasites in cardiac muscle tissue.
items.explanation: While both the assertion and reason are true, the presence of cysts and aggregates does not directly explain pericarditis.

items.assertion: Toxoplasmosis genotypes prevalent in South America are more virulent than those typically seen in North America or Europe.
items.reason: These genotypes may be associated with acute or recurrent ocular disease and other severe manifestations even in immunocompetent individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why South American genotypes are considered more virulent.

items.assertion: In immunocompromised patients, toxoplasmosis rarely involves the CNS.
items.reason: Toxoplasmosis is a principal opportunistic infection of the CNS in persons with AIDS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because toxoplasmosis frequently involves the CNS in immunocompromised patients.

items.assertion: Cerebral toxoplasmosis must be differentiated from other CNS infections in AIDS patients.
items.reason: The differential diagnosis includes herpes simplex encephalitis, cryptococcal meningitis, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, and primary CNS lymphoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why differentiation is necessary.

items.assertion: Toxoplasma pneumonia can be confused with Pneumocystis pneumonia.
items.reason: Both conditions present with dyspnea, fever, and a nonproductive cough.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why confusion may occur.

items.assertion: A positive IgM titer indicates acute toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent adults or children.
items.reason: IgM titers are used to diagnose acute infection when lymphadenopathy is present.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: IgG titers correlate with active toxoplasmosis infection.
items.reason: Active infection is often preceded by serologic evidence of T. gondii infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; IgG titers do not correlate with active infection.

items.assertion: Serum antibody titer may not correlate with active lesions in congenital toxoplasmosis.
items.reason: A positive IgG titer does not always indicate the presence of active lesions, particularly in congenital cases.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: Clinical improvement by day 7 in patients treated for toxoplasmosis encephalitis indicates a positive response to therapy.
items.reason: More than 90% of treated patients show evidence of improvement by day 7.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: TMP-SMX prophylaxis should be given after engraftment in allogeneic HSCT.
items.reason: The incidence of toxoplasmosis is lower in seropositive allogeneic HSCT with TMP-SMX prophylaxis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why TMP-SMX prophylaxis should be given.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.assertion: Prophylaxis against TE is recommended for AIDS patients with a CD4+ T lymphocyte count of >200/μL.
items.reason: AIDS patients with a CD4+ T lymphocyte count of <100/μL are at greatest risk for developing TE.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; prophylaxis is recommended for those with a CD4+ count <100/μL, not >200/μL.
items.reference: Toxoplasma Infections · INFECTION IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS
